titleStmt.title: Letter from Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery to Edward Carson, 9 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a copy of a letter from Hugh de Fellenberg Montgomery (1844-1924), Unionist and Liberal, to Edward Carson (1854-1935), Unionist leader. Carson was a lawyer and a politician.Carson was the leading figure in Ulster Unionism. He had sanctioned the formation of the paramilitary Ulster Volunteer Force in January 1913 and he helped to found the Ulster provisional government of September 1913. In 1916 he was at the peak of his parliamentary influence and by December was appointed to the first lordship of the admiralty by Lloyd George (1863-1945). Originally a Liberal and a strong supporter of Gladstone, Montgomery was also a firm Unionist, but by 1916 he believed that Ulster Unionists had no choice but to accept Lloyd George's proposal for a six-county Northern Ireland. The letter regards negotiations about the position of the six counties in relation to Irish Home Rule, stating that John Redmond (1856-1918), Irish Parliamentary Party leader, may repudiate his support for a six county plan. It goes on to discuss what Carson's position should be if invited into the negotiations, going on to discuss the threat of Home Rule. Montgomery also accounts his attendance at a meeting of the Unionist Alliance, at which Lloyd George MP (1883-1945), attended.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Copy 9th June 1916 My dear Carson, The contents of this day's Nationalist papers look as
if we might have a repudiation of Redmond's provisional
consent to the six county plan before Monday, which would
save us from a considerable difficulty. If this does not occur it will be obviously righ tactics
for us to authorise you to enter upon negotiations on the six
county basis. I hope, however, that you will arrange to have a resolution
proposed which will make it quite clear that we only do this
under great stress on account of the war, and that we are not
bound by what we do on Monday under any other circumstances
that may arise. The resolution should contain some plausible excuse
for breaking or stretching the terms of the Covenant, and (which
I think quite as important) for giving even an implied assent
to the setting up of Home Rule for any part of Ireland at the
present time, involving, as it does, most serious danger to the
Realm, and with which even implicit consent appears to me to
amount to an act of treason, which requires a very valid excuse.
I feel that it is as big a breach of my Oath of Allegiance as
the abandoning of the three counties is of the Covenant. I attended the Unionist Alliance meeting yersterday.
Some of them had been seeing Lloyd George, and had received certain
offers from him, after considering which, they passed the
resolution in to-day's papers. I got them to tone one part
of it, which I thought very unwise, down a little; but did
not try to prevent them issuing it. Not knowing, however,
what sort of resolution I might find myself obliged to vote
for on Monday, I left the room before the resolution was
put, and they have considerately omitted my name from the
list of those present. Yours very truly,
